feed.title: Pronounce — recent dictionary additions
feed.subtitle: How engineers actually pronounce project / product / jargon names. Community-maintained.

entry.title: Ornstein-Uhlenbeck — orn stine oo len bek
entry.summary: Ornstein-Uhlenbeck is pronounced "orn stine oo len bek" (/ˈɔːrnstaɪn ˈuːlənbɛk/). After Dutch physicists Leonard Ornstein and George Uhlenbeck; 'ORN-stine OO-len-bek', Uhlenbeck not 'YOO-len-bek'.

entry.title: Radon — ray don
entry.summary: Radon is pronounced "ray don" (/ˈreɪdɒn/). Austrian mathematician Johann Radon (Radon-Nikodym derivative); English 'RAY-don', German-faithful 'RAH-don' also heard.

entry.title: Lyapunov — lee ah puh nof
entry.summary: Lyapunov is pronounced "lee ah puh nof" (/liˈɑːpʊnɒf/). Aleksandr Lyapunov (stability theory); Russian, genuinely split between penultimate 'lee-AH-puh-nof' and original final-stress 'lyah-poo-NOF'.

entry.title: Noether — nur ter
entry.summary: Noether is pronounced "nur ter" (/ˈnɜːrtər/). Emmy Noether (Noether's theorem); German ö → 'NUR-ter', final th is a hard t, not English 'th'.

entry.title: Koopman — kohp mun
entry.summary: Koopman is pronounced "kohp mun" (/ˈkoʊpmən/). Bernard Koopman (Koopman operator); Dutch surname, long 'oh' → 'KOHP-man', not 'KOOP'.

entry.title: Hamiltonian — ham il toh nee un
entry.summary: Hamiltonian is pronounced "ham il toh nee un" (/ˌhæməlˈtoʊniən/). After W. R. Hamilton (Hamiltonian dynamics); 'ham-il-TOH-nee-un', stress on third syllable.

entry.title: Helmholtz — helm holts
entry.summary: Helmholtz is pronounced "helm holts" (/ˈhɛlmhoʊlts/). Hermann von Helmholtz (free energy, Helmholtz machine); German 'HELM-holts', tz → 'ts'.

entry.title: Planck — plahngk
entry.summary: Planck is pronounced "plahngk" (/ˈplɑːŋk/). Max Planck (Planck constant); German 'PLAHNGK', rhymes roughly with English 'plank'.

entry.title: Gumbel — gum bul
entry.summary: Gumbel is pronounced "gum bul" (/ˈɡʌmbəl/). After German statistician Emil Julius Gumbel (Gumbel-softmax); English 'GUM-bul' (rhymes with humble), German 'GOOM-bel'.

entry.title: Frobenius — froh bay nee us
entry.summary: Frobenius is pronounced "froh bay nee us" (/froʊˈbeɪniəs/). After German mathematician Ferdinand Georg Frobenius (Frobenius norm); German 'froh-BAY-nee-us', anglicized 'froh-BEE-nee-us'.

entry.title: Kullback-Leibler — kool bak ly bler
entry.summary: Kullback-Leibler is pronounced "kool bak ly bler" (/ˈkʊlbæk ˈlaɪblər/). After American mathematicians Solomon Kullback and Richard Leibler (KL divergence); 'KOOL-bak LYE-bler', Leibler 'ei'='eye'.

entry.title: Bregman — breg mun
entry.summary: Bregman is pronounced "breg mun" (/ˈbrɛɡmən/). After Soviet-Israeli mathematician Lev M. Bregman (Bregman divergence); Russian 'э'='eh', so 'BREG-man' not 'BRAYG-man'.

entry.title: Sinkhorn — sink horn
entry.summary: Sinkhorn is pronounced "sink horn" (/ˈsɪŋkhɔːrn/). After American mathematician Richard Sinkhorn (Sinkhorn iteration); straightforward 'SINK-horn'.

entry.title: Tsallis — sal iss
entry.summary: Tsallis is pronounced "sal iss" (/ˈsælɪs/). After Greek-Brazilian physicist Constantino Tsallis (Tsallis entropy); anglicized 'SAL-iss', Greek-faithful 'TSAH-liss'.

entry.title: Renyi — ray nyee
entry.summary: Renyi is pronounced "ray nyee" (/ˈreɪnjiː/). After Hungarian mathematician Alfréd Rényi (Rényi entropy); Hungarian é='ay', ny=palatal n, so 'RAY-nyee'.

entry.title: Riemannian — ree mah nee un
entry.summary: Riemannian is pronounced "ree mah nee un" (/riˈmɑːniən/). Adjective from German mathematician Bernhard Riemann (/ˈriːmɑːn/); 'ree-MAH-nee-un', not 'rye-'.

entry.title: Mahalanobis — mah huh luh noh bis
entry.summary: Mahalanobis is pronounced "mah huh luh noh bis" (/ˌmɑːhələˈnoʊbɪs/). Bengali surname of statistician P. C. Mahalanobis; stress on 'NOH', 'mah-huh-luh-NOH-bis'.

entry.title: Lipschitz — lip shits
entry.summary: Lipschitz is pronounced "lip shits" (/ˈlɪpʃɪts/). German mathematician Rudolf Lipschitz; German 'sch' gives 'LIP-shits', often Anglicized to 'LIP-skits'.

entry.title: Hilbert — hil bert
entry.summary: Hilbert is pronounced "hil bert" (/ˈhɪlbərt/). German mathematician David Hilbert; 'HIL-bert', stress on first syllable.

entry.title: Lebesgue — luh beg
entry.summary: Lebesgue is pronounced "luh beg" (/ləˈbɛɡ/). French mathematician Henri Lebesgue; silent '-gue' gives 'luh-BEG', not 'luh-BESG'.

entry.title: Minkowski — ming kawf skee
entry.summary: Minkowski is pronounced "ming kawf skee" (/mɪŋˈkɔːfski/). German mathematician Hermann Minkowski; German 'ming-KAWF-skee' vs common English 'ming-KOW-skee'.

entry.title: Chebyshev — cheb ih shoff
entry.summary: Chebyshev is pronounced "cheb ih shoff" (/ˈtʃɛbɪʃɔːf/). Russian Pafnuty Chebyshev; closest is 'CHEB-ih-shoff' (-off ending), but English math often says 'cheb-ih-SHEV'.

entry.title: Hadamard — had uh mar
entry.summary: Hadamard is pronounced "had uh mar" (/ˌhædəˈmɑːr/). French Jacques Hadamard, [adamaʁ] (silent final d); English speakers split between 'had-uh-MAR' and anglicized 'HAD-uh-mard'.

entry.title: Kronecker — kroh neck er
entry.summary: Kronecker is pronounced "kroh neck er" (/ˈkroʊnɛkər/). German Leopold Kronecker, [ˈkʁoːnɛkɐ]; 'KROH-neck-er' with stress on first syllable, not 'kro-NECK-er'.

entry.title: Toeplitz — tope lits
entry.summary: Toeplitz is pronounced "tope lits" (/ˈtoʊplɪts/). German Otto Toeplitz, [ˈtœplɪts] (oe = ö); English usually anglicized 'TOPE-lits', also heard 'TEP-lits'.
